ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic scrambled egg food product entry; no specific Indian spiced scrambled egg (bhurji) entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Indian spiced scrambled eggs. A0CKP (hen egg, cooked) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for egg bhurji or Indian spiced scrambled eggs.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of egg bhurji is approximately 200 g, typically comprising two to three scrambled eggs cooked with onion, tomato, capsicum, and spices, served alongside bread or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Bhurji (Scrambled Egg with Spices) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for cooked egg preparations including scrambled eggs with vegetables and spices. Values for egg bhurji are adapted from the ICMR-NIN scrambled egg with vegetables entry, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 748967, Scrambled Egg) for macro validation. Per-100g values reflect a typical egg bhurji composition with onion, tomato, capsicum, and oil.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for egg bhurji in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 30 based on the analogous food 'scrambled eggs with vegetables' which is a low-GI egg-dominant dish. Eggs have a negligible GI (approximately 0) and the vegetable and spice additions (onion, tomato, capsicum) contribute minimal glycemic carbohydrates. The GI of 30 is a conservative estimate anchored to egg-based cooked dishes in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. GL per serving = (30 × 5.4 × 200/100) / 100 = 3.24, rounded to 3.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g) from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size as a 200 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 620 mg sodium; request reduced salt preparation and avoid pairing with high-sodium breads or chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low net carbohydrate content (~5.4 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg bhurji is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, high protein content, and minimal carbohydrate load; a standard 200 g serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 3.2, which is well within safe limits, though oil quantity should be moderated to manage total caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol (~285 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (~3.1 g per 100 g) from whole eggs and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit egg bhurji to two to three servings per week, preferring preparation with minimal oil or ghee, as the dish provides significant dietary cholesterol from whole eggs; the vegetable content (tomato, capsicum, onion) contributes beneficial antioxidants and fibre that partially offset cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Bhurji is a popular Indian spiced scrambled egg dish prepared by stir-frying beaten whole eggs with sautéed onions, tomatoes, capsicum (bell pepper), green chillies, and a blend of spices including cumin, turmeric, red chilli powder, and garam masala, finished with fresh coriander leaves. The dish has a soft, moist, and slightly chunky texture with a bold, savoury, and mildly spicy flavour profile. It is a staple street food and home-cooked dish across India, commonly served with pav (soft bread rolls), roti, paratha, or rice. In Singapore, it appears on Indian restaurant menus as a side dish or main protein component. Nutritionally, egg bhurji is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish with a low glycemic index of approximately 30, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. Each 100 g provides approximately 168 kcal, 10.2 g protein, 11.8 g fat, and 5.4 g carbohydrate. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, choline (from eggs), vitamin C (from capsicum and tomato), and iron. The sodium content is moderate at approximately 310 mg per 100 g and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension. Egg bhurji is naturally gluten-free and halal-compliant when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg bhurji?
Is egg bhurji good for diabetes?
What is the difference between egg bhurji and omelette?
Is egg bhurji high in protein?
Can I eat egg bhurji if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in egg bhurji?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN scrambled egg with vegetables data and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central scrambled egg entries. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for egg bhurji with capsicum exists; values are adapted from the closest matching preparation. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to egg-based cooked dishes in peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) scrambled egg with vegetables entry and cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 748967. No single exact source entry for egg bhurji with capsicum in tomato-onion masala exists in approved databases; values represent a defensible adaptation. 2. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for egg bhurji exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is anchored to egg-dominant cooked dishes in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins are estimated from USDA FoodData Central scrambled egg with vegetables data and are marked as adapted. 4. The input record listed the food as 'Egg Bhurji / Omelette'; the canonical food_name has been set to 'Egg Bhurji' as the description clearly describes a bhurji (scrambled) preparation, not an omelette. 5. Serving size of 200 g is a standard restaurant portion estimate; actual restaurant portions may vary. 6. The food is served at Zaffron Tandoori, Boat Quay, Singapore, per the field survey source record.
